question: Explain the significance of the Halting Problem in the Theory of Computation.
expectedPoints: The Halting Problem is the problem of determining whether an arbitrary program will finish running or continue to run forever.
Alan Turing proved it is undecidable, meaning no algorithm exists that can solve it for all possible program-input pairs.
This result is fundamental as it places a concrete limit on what can be computed, establishing the existence of problems beyond the reach of any computational model (like a Turing Machine).

question: Differentiate between a decidable language and a Turing-recognizable (recursively enumerable) language.
expectedPoints: A language is decidable if there exists a Turing Machine that will accept every string in the language, reject every string not in the language, and is guaranteed to halt on all inputs.
A language is Turing-recognizable if there exists a Turing Machine that will accept every string in the language and either reject or loop forever on strings not in the language.
All decidable languages are Turing-recognizable, but not all Turing-recognizable languages are decidable (e.g., the Halting Problem language is recognizable but not decidable).

question: Describe the Church-Turing thesis and its implications. Furthermore, discuss how the concept of reducibility is used to classify computational problems.
expectedPoints: The Church-Turing thesis is a hypothesis stating that any function that can be computed by an algorithm can be computed by a Turing Machine. It equates the intuitive notion of an 'effective method' with the formal definition of a Turing Machine.
Its implication is that the Turing Machine is a universal model of computation, forming the foundation for understanding the limits of what can be computed.

Reducibility is a technique used to compare the complexity of problems. If problem A can be reduced to problem B, then a solution to B can be used to solve A. This allows us to prove a problem is undecidable by reducing a known undecidable problem (like the Halting Problem) to it, or to classify its complexity (e.g., NP-completeness via polynomial-time reducibility).
